name: E-commerce / Marketplace
description: E-commerce and marketplace platforms enable businesses and individuals to buy and sell goods or services digitally, connecting buyers with sellers at scale. These platforms manage the full transaction lifecycle from product discovery and cart to payment, fulfillment, and post-purchase support. Modern e-commerce solutions blend storefront personalization, logistics orchestration, and seller tooling into unified digital commerce ecosystems.
commonRisks: Payment fraud and chargebacks
Inventory stockouts or overselling
Seller trust and counterfeit goods
Platform downtime during peak traffic
Data privacy breaches
Regulatory non-compliance (sales tax, cross-border)
complianceConsiderations: PCI DSS compliance for payment data
GDPR / CCPA for customer data privacy
Sales tax nexus and remittance laws
Consumer protection and return policy regulations
AML/KYC for marketplace seller onboarding
starterPromptContext: You are working on an e-commerce or marketplace platform. The team focuses on improving buyer conversion, seller growth, and operational efficiency across listing, checkout, fulfillment, and post-purchase workflows. Stakeholders include merchants, buyers, and internal operations teams, and success is measured by GMV, conversion rate, and seller satisfaction.

name: Finance / FinTech
description: FinTech covers a broad range of technology-driven financial services including digital banking, lending, investing, payments, and personal finance management. Products in this space must balance regulatory compliance, security, and user trust while delivering frictionless digital experiences. Innovation cycles are often governed by licensing requirements, partner bank relationships, and evolving regulatory frameworks.
commonRisks: Regulatory non-compliance (BSA, AML, CFPB)
Fraud and identity theft
Third-party vendor concentration risk
Data breach and PII exposure
Model risk in credit decisioning
Operational failures in payment rails
complianceConsiderations: Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) and AML program requirements
CFPB regulations for consumer lending and UDAAP
SEC/FINRA rules for investment products
GDPR / CCPA for financial data privacy
SOX compliance for internal financial controls
starterPromptContext: You are working in the FinTech or financial services domain where regulatory compliance, security, and trust are non-negotiable. Products must balance user experience innovation with strict requirements from banking regulators, risk management teams, and compliance officers. Success is measured by transaction volume, customer growth, default rates, and audit readiness.

name: Healthcare
description: Healthcare technology spans electronic health records, patient portals, clinical decision support, telehealth, and administrative systems that support care delivery. These products must navigate complex clinical workflows, strict regulatory requirements, and interoperability standards while improving patient outcomes and operational efficiency. The domain involves multiple stakeholders including clinicians, administrators, payers, and patients, each with distinct needs and data access requirements.
commonWorkflows: Care plan creation and care coordination
Discharge planning and transitions of care
commonRisks: HIPAA violation and PHI data breach
Clinical data errors causing patient safety issues
Interoperability failures between EHR systems
Regulatory non-compliance with CMS rules
Vendor lock-in for EHR platforms
Clinician burnout from poor UX and documentation burden
complianceConsiderations: HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules for PHI
21st Century Cures Act and information blocking rules
HL7 FHIR interoperability standards
CMS quality reporting and value-based care requirements
FDA regulations for clinical decision support tools
starterPromptContext: You are working in the healthcare domain where patient safety, data privacy, and regulatory compliance are the highest priorities. Products must integrate with clinical workflows, EHR systems, and payer processes while improving care quality and reducing administrative burden. Key stakeholders include clinicians, hospital administrators, payers, and patients.

name: Insurance
description: The insurance domain encompasses technology platforms supporting policy underwriting, claims management, customer servicing, and actuarial analysis across life, health, property, and casualty lines. Insurers must manage complex product configurations, regulatory filings, and claims workflows while modernizing legacy core systems. Digital transformation in insurance focuses on improving customer experience, accelerating claims settlement, and enabling data-driven underwriting.
commonWorkflows: First Notice of Loss (FNOL) and claims intake
commonRisks: Fraudulent claims and organized fraud rings
Regulatory non-compliance with state insurance commissioners
Catastrophic loss events (CAT) overwhelming claims capacity
Legacy core system limitations slowing modernization
Data quality issues affecting actuarial models
Cybersecurity threats to sensitive policyholder data
complianceConsiderations: State insurance department regulations and rate filings
NAIC model laws and interstate compact requirements
GDPR / state privacy laws for policyholder data
SOX controls for public insurers
Anti-fraud program requirements
starterPromptContext: You are working in the insurance domain on platforms supporting underwriting, claims, and policy management. The business prioritizes fast claims settlement, fraud prevention, and regulatory compliance while modernizing outdated core systems. Key stakeholders include underwriters, claims adjusters, agents, and regulators.

name: Manufacturing
description: Manufacturing technology encompasses MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems), ERP integration, quality management, and shop floor digitization to optimize production operations. Smart manufacturing initiatives combine IoT sensor data, predictive maintenance, and digital twins to reduce downtime and improve throughput. Products in this space must connect plant-floor operational technology (OT) with enterprise IT systems while supporting lean and continuous improvement methodologies.
commonRisks: Unplanned equipment downtime
Quality escapes reaching customers
Supply chain disruptions affecting raw materials
Cybersecurity vulnerabilities in OT networks
Worker safety incidents
Regulatory non-compliance (ISO, OSHA)
complianceConsiderations: ISO 9001 quality management standards
OSHA workplace safety regulations
Environmental regulations (EPA, ISO 14001)
Industry-specific standards (IATF 16949 for automotive, FDA for medical devices)
Export control and trade compliance (ITAR, EAR)
starterPromptContext: You are working in manufacturing technology, focused on digitizing shop floor operations, improving OEE, and connecting plant systems to enterprise IT. Key stakeholders include plant operations, quality, maintenance, and supply chain teams. Success is measured by production throughput, quality yield, and equipment uptime.

name: Retail
description: Retail technology powers physical store operations, omnichannel commerce, and customer engagement for brands selling through brick-and-mortar, online, and hybrid channels. Modern retail platforms integrate point-of-sale systems, inventory management, loyalty programs, and store associate tooling to deliver consistent shopping experiences. The domain is shaped by consumer expectations for seamless online-to-offline experiences, real-time inventory visibility, and personalized offers.
commonMetrics: Basket size and items per transaction
commonRisks: Inventory out-of-stock or overstock situations
POS system downtime during peak sales
Shoplifting and organized retail crime
Omnichannel fulfillment failures
Data privacy breaches of loyalty program data
Supply chain disruptions impacting store replenishment
complianceConsiderations: PCI DSS for payment data at POS
ADA accessibility requirements for digital touchpoints
Consumer data privacy laws (CCPA, GDPR)
FTC promotional pricing and advertising rules
Employment laws for shift scheduling and labor management
starterPromptContext: You are working in retail technology where the goal is delivering seamless omnichannel shopping experiences across stores and digital channels. Key stakeholders include merchandising, store operations, loss prevention, and e-commerce teams. Success is measured by sales conversion, inventory accuracy, customer loyalty, and operational efficiency.

name: Logistics
description: Logistics technology platforms manage the movement, tracking, and optimization of goods from origin to destination across transportation, warehousing, and last-mile delivery networks. Modern logistics systems leverage route optimization, real-time visibility, and carrier management to reduce costs and improve delivery reliability. The domain is highly data-intensive, requiring integration across carriers, customs systems, WMS, and customer order management.
commonWorkflows: Real-time shipment tracking and event management
Last-mile delivery scheduling and proof of delivery
Carrier invoice audit and freight payment
commonRisks: Carrier capacity shortages and rate volatility
Shipment delays due to weather or disruptions
Customs holds and trade compliance violations
Warehouse labor shortages
Cargo theft and loss
System integration failures between WMS and TMS
complianceConsiderations: DOT and FMCSA regulations for trucking
Customs and CBP import/export regulations
Hazardous materials (HAZMAT) shipping requirements
GDPR/CCPA for customer shipment data
CTPAT and trade compliance program requirements
starterPromptContext: You are working in logistics technology focused on shipment visibility, carrier management, warehouse operations, and last-mile delivery. Key stakeholders include logistics planners, warehouse managers, carriers, and customer-facing operations teams. Success metrics include on-time delivery, cost per shipment, and fulfillment cycle time.

name: Automotive
description: Automotive technology spans connected vehicle platforms, dealer management systems, electric vehicle infrastructure, and manufacturing operations for the global auto industry. The sector is undergoing rapid transformation driven by electrification, autonomous driving, over-the-air software updates, and digital retail of vehicles. Products must serve OEMs, dealers, fleet operators, and consumers while meeting stringent safety and regulatory standards.
commonWorkflows: Service appointment scheduling and repair order management
commonRisks: Vehicle safety recalls and NHTSA investigations
Software OTA update failures causing vehicle issues
Cybersecurity vulnerabilities in connected vehicles
Supply chain disruptions (semiconductor shortages)
Dealer channel conflict in direct-to-consumer shift
EV infrastructure immaturity affecting customer experience
complianceConsiderations: NHTSA Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards (FMVSS)
UNECE vehicle type approval regulations
ISO 26262 functional safety for automotive software
UNECE WP.29 cybersecurity regulations for connected vehicles
EPA emissions and CAFE fuel economy standards
starterPromptContext: You are working in the automotive technology sector on connected vehicles, dealer platforms, or EV infrastructure. The domain requires balancing rapid software innovation with strict vehicle safety and regulatory requirements. Key stakeholders include OEM engineering teams, dealerships, fleet operators, and regulatory bodies.

name: SaaS
description: Software-as-a-Service products are cloud-delivered applications serving business or consumer customers through subscription models, requiring continuous delivery of value through product iterations. SaaS companies must balance feature velocity with platform reliability, security, and scalability while managing complex multi-tenant architectures. Success depends on driving adoption, reducing churn, expanding accounts, and maintaining high availability SLAs.
commonWorkflows: Customer health scoring and churn prediction
commonMetrics: Time to value (TTV) for new customers
commonRisks: High customer churn due to poor adoption
Platform outages affecting SLA commitments
Security breaches in multi-tenant environments
Feature bloat reducing product usability
Pricing pressure from competitors
Integration failures with enterprise customer systems
commonIntegrations: Billing and subscription platforms (Stripe, Chargebee, Zuora)
complianceConsiderations: SOC 2 Type II certification for enterprise customers
GDPR / CCPA data privacy compliance
ISO 27001 information security standard
HIPAA if serving healthcare customers
Accessibility standards (WCAG 2.1) for enterprise customers
starterPromptContext: You are working on a SaaS product where the core challenge is driving user adoption, reducing churn, and growing net revenue retention. The team must balance new feature development with platform reliability and enterprise customer requirements. Success is measured by ARR growth, churn rate, feature adoption, and time to value.

name: AI / Data Platforms
description: AI and data platform products provide infrastructure, tooling, and services that enable organizations to build, train, deploy, and monitor machine learning models and data pipelines at scale. This domain includes MLOps platforms, data warehouses, business intelligence tools, and AI application development frameworks. Products must serve both technical data scientists and engineers as well as business users seeking AI-powered insights.
commonWorkflows: Feature engineering and feature store management
Model training, tuning, and experiment tracking
AI model governance and bias assessment
commonRisks: Model bias and fairness issues in production
Data quality issues corrupting model outputs
AI hallucinations in generative AI features
Regulatory risk from AI decisions (ECOA, GDPR AI Act)
Cost overruns in compute infrastructure
Data governance failures and lineage gaps
commonIntegrations: Cloud platforms (AWS SageMaker, Azure ML, GCP Vertex AI)
Vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate) for LLM applications
complianceConsiderations: EU AI Act requirements for high-risk AI systems
GDPR right to explanation for automated decisions
ECOA and fair lending requirements for credit AI models
SOC 2 for platform data security
Model governance and internal AI ethics policies
starterPromptContext: You are working on an AI or data platform product serving data scientists, ML engineers, and business analytics users. The platform must deliver reliable, scalable, and trustworthy AI/ML capabilities with strong data governance. Key success metrics include model deployment velocity, data pipeline reliability, and adoption by data teams.

name: Education / EdTech
description: Education technology platforms support learning delivery, student management, and educational content for K-12 schools, higher education institutions, and corporate learning environments. Modern EdTech combines adaptive learning algorithms, learning analytics, and digital content management to personalize educational experiences. Products must serve learners, educators, administrators, and parents while meeting accessibility and student data privacy requirements.
commonWorkflows: Video lecture and live session management
commonRisks: Student data privacy violations (FERPA, COPPA)
Accessibility gaps for learners with disabilities
Low course completion and learner dropout
Academic integrity issues (cheating, plagiarism)
Digital divide and device/internet access inequity
Content licensing and copyright compliance
complianceConsiderations: FERPA for student education record privacy
COPPA for children under 13
WCAG 2.1 accessibility standards
State-specific student data privacy laws
Section 508 for federal or government-funded institutions
starterPromptContext: You are working on an EdTech platform serving students, educators, and administrators in K-12, higher education, or corporate learning contexts. The core challenge is improving learner engagement, course completion, and measurable learning outcomes while meeting strict data privacy and accessibility requirements.

name: Real Estate / PropTech
description: PropTech encompasses digital platforms for property search, transaction management, property management, and real estate investment analytics. The sector is transforming traditional brokerage, leasing, and property operations through digital workflows, data-driven valuations, and IoT-connected building management. Products serve buyers, sellers, landlords, tenants, investors, and brokerages navigating complex real estate transactions and property portfolios.
commonWorkflows: Online tour scheduling and virtual tours
commonRisks: Fair housing law violations in listing algorithms
Data privacy risks for financial and personal tenant data
Real estate market volatility affecting platform usage
MLS data access and licensing compliance
Fraud in rental listings and transactions
Regulatory changes to short-term rental rules
complianceConsiderations: Fair Housing Act and anti-discrimination requirements
State-specific real estate licensing laws
RESPA for real estate transactions
Tenant data privacy regulations
Short-term rental regulations and city zoning compliance
starterPromptContext: You are working in PropTech where the goal is to digitize and streamline property search, transactions, leasing, and property management. Key stakeholders include agents, tenants, landlords, investors, and property managers. Success is measured by transaction volume, tenant retention, occupancy rates, and platform adoption.

name: Travel / Hospitality
description: Travel and hospitality technology powers booking, operations, and guest experience management for airlines, hotels, cruise lines, vacation rentals, and online travel agencies (OTAs). Platforms in this domain manage complex inventory, pricing, and distribution across global reservation systems while delivering personalized travel experiences. The sector demands real-time availability management, dynamic pricing engines, and seamless customer communication throughout the travel journey.
commonUsers: Hotel and Property Front Desk Staff
commonWorkflows: Property or flight search and availability check
commonMetrics: Revenue Per Available Room (RevPAR) for hotels
commonRisks: Demand volatility due to economic or geopolitical events
Overbooking and inventory management failures
Data breaches of traveler PII and payment data
Regulatory compliance across international travel laws
OTA dependency and commission cost pressure
Operational disruptions (weather, labor strikes)
complianceConsiderations: GDPR for European traveler data
PCI DSS for payment processing
Accessibility requirements (ADA for US properties)
Aviation regulations (DOT, IATA)
Local tourism and accommodation licensing laws
starterPromptContext: You are working in travel and hospitality technology where delivering seamless guest experiences and maximizing revenue per available inventory unit are top priorities. Key stakeholders include revenue managers, operations staff, distribution teams, and guests. Success is measured by booking conversion, RevPAR, guest satisfaction, and direct booking growth.

name: Media / Entertainment
description: Media and entertainment technology supports content creation, distribution, streaming, and audience monetization for publishers, studios, broadcasters, and streaming services. The sector is driven by the shift to direct-to-consumer (DTC) streaming, creator economy platforms, and AI-powered content recommendation. Products must manage rights and licensing complexity, content moderation at scale, and personalization across massive global audiences.
commonWorkflows: Ad inventory management and programmatic selling
Content moderation and trust and safety
commonMetrics: Daily Active Users (DAU) and Monthly Active Users (MAU)
commonRisks: Content piracy and unauthorized distribution
Rights licensing violations and copyright disputes
Harmful content and moderation failures
Subscriber churn due to content gaps
CDN costs and streaming infrastructure scaling
Ad fraud and invalid traffic
commonIntegrations: Ad tech platforms (Google Ad Manager, The Trade Desk)
complianceConsiderations: DMCA copyright compliance and takedown processes
GDPR / CCPA for audience data
FTC requirements for sponsored content disclosure
COPPA for children's content platforms
Accessibility requirements (closed captions, audio description)
starterPromptContext: You are working in media and entertainment technology on content platforms, streaming services, or creator tools. Key challenges include reducing subscriber churn, improving content discovery through personalization, and scaling content operations. Success is measured by engagement time, subscriber growth, and content conversion.

name: Telecom
description: Telecommunications technology covers network infrastructure management, subscriber lifecycle management, and digital service delivery for mobile operators, ISPs, and enterprise connectivity providers. Telecom companies are investing heavily in 5G, fiber, and cloud-native network functions while modernizing BSS/OSS stacks to improve customer self-service and reduce operational costs. Products must handle massive subscriber volumes, real-time network events, and complex pricing and plan management.
commonWorkflows: Customer service escalation and case management
Regulatory reporting and lawful intercept compliance
commonRisks: Network outages affecting large subscriber bases
Regulatory fines from FCC / Ofcom non-compliance
SIM swap fraud and identity theft
Revenue leakage from billing errors
5G network security vulnerabilities
Market share loss from MVNOs and digital disruptors
complianceConsiderations: FCC regulations for US carriers (CPNI, E911)
Lawful intercept and CALEA compliance
GDPR / privacy regulations for subscriber data
TCPA for outbound marketing and messaging
Net neutrality and open internet regulations
starterPromptContext: You are working in telecommunications technology covering subscriber management, network operations, and digital service delivery. The business priority is reducing churn, growing ARPU, and modernizing BSS/OSS systems. Key stakeholders include network operations, customer service, regulatory, and digital product teams.

name: Energy / Utilities
description: Energy and utilities technology platforms support grid management, metering, customer information systems, and renewable energy integration for electric, gas, and water utilities. The energy transition is driving investment in smart grid technology, EV charging infrastructure, demand response programs, and distributed energy resource (DER) management. Products must balance grid reliability, regulatory compliance, and customer engagement across residential, commercial, and industrial segments.
commonWorkflows: Smart meter reading and data collection
Rate case filing and regulatory reporting
commonMetrics: Renewable energy percentage of generation mix
commonRisks: Grid cyberattacks on critical infrastructure
Major weather events causing widespread outages
Regulatory non-compliance with NERC CIP standards
DER and solar integration instability on the grid
Revenue erosion from energy efficiency and prosumers
Legacy SCADA and operational technology vulnerabilities
complianceConsiderations: NERC CIP cybersecurity standards for bulk electric system
FERC regulations for wholesale electricity markets
State Public Utility Commission (PUC) regulations
EPA Clean Air Act and environmental reporting
GDPR / state privacy laws for customer energy data
starterPromptContext: You are working in the energy and utilities sector on smart grid, customer information, or field operations platforms. The key challenges include grid reliability, renewable energy integration, and regulatory compliance while modernizing aging infrastructure. Success is measured by outage frequency, customer satisfaction, and operational efficiency.

name: Government / Public Sector
description: Government technology platforms deliver digital services to citizens, support public administration operations, and modernize legacy government systems across federal, state, and local agencies. The sector is driven by digital government initiatives aimed at improving service accessibility, reducing processing times, and increasing transparency. Products must meet rigorous security, accessibility, and procurement standards while serving diverse public users with varying digital literacy levels.
commonWorkflows: Government data publishing and open data
Internal workflow automation and document management
commonMetrics: Error and rework rate on applications
System uptime during peak filing periods
commonRisks: Data breaches of sensitive citizen PII
Accessibility failures excluding disabled users
Procurement delays and vendor lock-in
Legacy system integration failures
Low digital adoption among vulnerable populations
Political and policy changes affecting product direction
commonIntegrations: State and federal identity verification systems
complianceConsiderations: Section 508 and WCAG 2.1 accessibility requirements
FedRAMP authorization for federal cloud systems
FISMA information security requirements
Privacy Act for federal agencies handling citizen PII
State-specific open records and FOIA requirements
starterPromptContext: You are working on government technology products that deliver digital services to citizens and support public administration operations. The highest priorities are security, accessibility, and compliance with government procurement standards. Success is measured by service adoption, processing efficiency, and citizen satisfaction.

name: Legal / LegalTech
description: LegalTech platforms support law firms, corporate legal departments, and legal service providers with contract management, e-discovery, matter management, and legal research tools. The sector is transforming through AI-powered contract analysis, automated document generation, and legal workflow automation. Products must handle highly confidential legal documents and communications while meeting strict privilege, chain-of-custody, and bar compliance requirements.
commonRisks: Confidentiality and attorney-client privilege breaches
AI hallucinations in legal research or contract analysis
Chain-of-custody failures in eDiscovery
Regulatory changes affecting legal practice
Bar association rules on attorney technology use
Data residency requirements for cross-border legal matters
complianceConsiderations: ABA Model Rules of Professional Conduct for attorney technology
State bar association ethics opinions on AI tools
GDPR and client data privacy obligations
Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (FRCP) for eDiscovery
Data residency requirements for cross-border matters
starterPromptContext: You are working in LegalTech developing platforms for law firms or corporate legal departments. The domain demands strict confidentiality, AI accuracy, and compliance with legal ethics rules. Key challenges include reducing contract cycle time, managing eDiscovery costs, and driving attorney adoption of new tools.

name: HR / Workforce Management
description: HR and workforce management technology supports the full employee lifecycle from talent acquisition and onboarding through performance management, learning, compensation, and offboarding. Modern HR platforms combine HRIS core systems with employee experience tools, people analytics, and AI-powered talent intelligence. Products must serve both HR administrators and employees as self-service users while integrating with payroll, benefits, and compliance systems.
commonRisks: Bias in AI-powered recruitment and performance tools
Employee data privacy breaches
Non-compliance with labor laws (FLSA, FMLA, EEOC)
Poor adoption leading to shadow HR processes
Integration failures between HRIS and payroll
Inaccurate people data affecting business decisions
complianceConsiderations: EEOC and anti-discrimination requirements
FLSA and wage and hour compliance
FMLA and leave management requirements
GDPR / CCPA for employee personal data
OFCCP affirmative action requirements for federal contractors
starterPromptContext: You are working on HR and workforce management technology covering the full employee lifecycle. Key challenges include improving recruiter efficiency, employee self-service adoption, and people analytics capabilities. Success is measured by time to fill, turnover reduction, system adoption, and compliance with labor regulations.

name: Cybersecurity
description: Cybersecurity technology products protect organizations from digital threats through detection, prevention, response, and recovery capabilities across endpoints, networks, cloud, and application layers. This includes SIEM, SOAR, EDR, identity and access management, and vulnerability management platforms. Product development in this space requires deep understanding of the threat landscape, compliance requirements, and the workflows of security operations center (SOC) analysts.
commonWorkflows: Identity and access provisioning and de-provisioning
Penetration testing and red team exercises
Compliance evidence collection and audit support
commonMetrics: SOC analyst capacity and alert volume
commonRisks: Zero-day exploits targeting platform infrastructure
Alert fatigue reducing SOC analyst effectiveness
Incomplete asset visibility creating blind spots
Insider threats and privileged access abuse
Third-party supply chain compromise
Regulatory non-compliance (PCI DSS, HIPAA, NIST)
commonIntegrations: Ticketing and SOAR platforms (ServiceNow, Palo Alto XSOAR)
complianceConsiderations: NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) alignment
PCI DSS for cardholder data security
HIPAA Security Rule for healthcare
SOC 2 Type II for service organizations
CMMC for US Department of Defense contractors
starterPromptContext: You are working in cybersecurity product development building tools for SOC analysts, incident responders, or security administrators. The primary challenge is reducing MTTD and MTTR while managing alert fatigue and improving analyst productivity. Key stakeholders include CISOs, SOC leaders, IT administrators, and compliance teams.

name: Supply Chain
description: Supply chain technology platforms enable end-to-end visibility, planning, and execution across procurement, manufacturing, inventory, and logistics networks spanning multiple tiers of suppliers. Modern supply chain systems incorporate real-time demand sensing, AI-powered planning, and supplier collaboration to improve resilience and reduce working capital. The domain requires integration across ERPs, supplier systems, and logistics platforms spanning global networks.
commonWorkflows: Purchase order creation and supplier management
Inventory replenishment and safety stock optimization
Supply chain exception and disruption management
commonMetrics: Supply chain cost as a percentage of revenue
commonRisks: Single-source supplier concentration risk
Geopolitical disruptions affecting supply routes
Demand volatility and forecast misalignment
Counterfeit components in the supply chain
Trade compliance and sanctions violations
Data quality gaps across multi-tier supplier networks
complianceConsiderations: Export control regulations (ITAR, EAR)
Conflict minerals reporting (Dodd-Frank Section 1502)
Modern slavery and supply chain transparency laws
ISO 28000 supply chain security management
CTPAT and AEO trusted trader programs
starterPromptContext: You are working in supply chain technology focused on demand planning, supplier management, and end-to-end supply chain visibility. Key challenges include improving forecast accuracy, reducing inventory, and building supply chain resilience. Success is measured by perfect order rate, inventory turnover, and supplier performance.

name: Construction
description: Construction technology platforms support project management, field operations, and financial management for general contractors, subcontractors, and project owners building commercial, residential, and infrastructure projects. The sector is undergoing digital transformation through BIM adoption, connected job sites, and integrated project management platforms replacing siloed spreadsheets and paper processes. Products must support distributed field teams with mobile-first tools while integrating with estimating, accounting, and design systems.
commonMetrics: Rework cost as a percentage of total cost
commonRisks: Project schedule delays and cost overruns
Worker safety incidents on job sites
Subcontractor financial default or non-performance
Design errors and rework
Supply chain delays for materials
Regulatory non-compliance with local building codes
commonIntegrations: Accounting and ERP (Sage 300 CRE, Viewpoint Vista)
Field management platforms (Procore, Autodesk Construction Cloud)
complianceConsiderations: OSHA construction safety regulations (29 CFR 1926)
Local building codes and permit requirements
Davis-Bacon prevailing wage requirements on public projects
LEED and sustainability certification requirements
AIA contract requirements for public-private projects
starterPromptContext: You are working in construction technology supporting project management, field operations, and financial management for contractors and project owners. The primary challenge is giving distributed field teams real-time visibility into project status, RFIs, submittals, and safety. Success is measured by schedule adherence, cost control, and safety incident rates.

name: Food / Restaurant Tech
description: Food and restaurant technology covers platforms for online ordering, point-of-sale systems, kitchen display management, delivery logistics, and restaurant operations management. The sector spans fast-casual, full-service dining, ghost kitchens, and food delivery marketplaces with distinct operational requirements for each model. Products must integrate ordering, kitchen operations, and delivery workflows while supporting loyalty programs and data-driven menu optimization.
commonWorkflows: Kitchen order routing and display management
commonRisks: POS system outages during peak dining hours
Food safety incidents and FDA violations
Delivery driver availability shortages
Third-party delivery platform dependency
High staff turnover disrupting operations
Menu pricing errors on delivery platforms
commonIntegrations: Delivery platforms (DoorDash Drive, Uber Eats for Business)
complianceConsiderations: FDA food safety and labeling regulations
Local health department permits and inspections
ADA accessibility for ordering kiosks and apps
Alcohol service compliance (age verification)
PCI DSS for card payments at POS and online
starterPromptContext: You are working in food and restaurant technology covering online ordering, POS, kitchen operations, and delivery logistics. The core challenge is delivering fast, accurate orders while improving operator profitability through data-driven menu and labor decisions. Key stakeholders are restaurant operators, kitchen staff, and delivery customers.

name: Nonprofit / Social Impact
description: Nonprofit and social impact technology platforms support fundraising, donor management, program delivery, and impact measurement for charitable organizations, NGOs, foundations, and social enterprises. These organizations operate with constrained budgets and must demonstrate measurable social impact to funders and boards. Products must serve mission-driven organizations with diverse technology maturity levels while meeting grant compliance and financial reporting requirements.
commonMetrics: Fundraising ROI (cost to raise a dollar)
commonRisks: Donor data privacy breaches
Grant compliance failures leading to fund clawbacks
Volunteer and staff burnout from limited resources
Overdependence on a few large donors
Technology adoption barriers with non-technical staff
Impact measurement gaps reducing funder credibility
commonIntegrations: CRM and donor management (Salesforce NPSP, Bloomerang)
complianceConsiderations: IRS 501(c)(3) reporting and unrelated business income
Grant compliance with federal Uniform Guidance (2 CFR 200)
State charitable solicitation registration requirements
GDPR / CCPA for donor data
Donor privacy and gift anonymity requirements
starterPromptContext: You are working on nonprofit technology platforms supporting fundraising, donor management, and social impact measurement. Organizations operate with limited budgets and must demonstrate measurable outcomes to donors and funders. Key stakeholders include development teams, program staff, grant managers, and board members.

name: Consumer Mobile Apps
description: Consumer mobile app products span social, utility, fitness, entertainment, and personal productivity categories, serving mass-market audiences through iOS and Android platforms. Success in consumer mobile requires rapid iteration, viral growth mechanics, and deep engagement instrumentation to optimize retention and monetization. Products operate in a highly competitive attention economy where activation, retention, and lifetime value are the primary growth levers.
commonWorkflows: App store review and rating management
commonMetrics: App store rating and review volume
commonRisks: App store policy violations causing removal
Low Day 1 retention killing growth
Negative viral moments damaging brand reputation
iOS and Android platform API changes breaking functionality
User data privacy violations (GDPR, COPPA, ATT)
Subscription churn from poor onboarding
commonIntegrations: App store APIs (Apple App Store Connect, Google Play Console)
Payment platforms (Apple Pay, Google Pay, RevenueCat)
A/B testing tools (Optimizely, Firebase Remote Config)
complianceConsiderations: Apple App Store Review Guidelines
Google Play Policy requirements
GDPR and ATT (App Tracking Transparency) compliance
COPPA for apps targeting children under 13
ADA accessibility guidelines for mobile apps
starterPromptContext: You are working on a consumer mobile app in a competitive attention-driven market. Key challenges include improving Day 1 activation, 30-day retention, and monetization conversion. The team must run rapid experiments and use behavioral analytics to guide decisions. Success is measured by DAU, retention curves, and LTV.

name: Payroll / HR Operations
description: Payroll and HR operations platforms automate the calculation, processing, and compliance of employee compensation including wages, taxes, benefits, deductions, and reporting across multiple jurisdictions. These systems are mission-critical infrastructure requiring near-perfect accuracy, as errors directly impact employee livelihoods and create regulatory liability. Modern payroll platforms are modernizing toward continuous or on-demand pay, real-time tax calculation, and global payroll consolidation.
commonWorkflows: Year-end W-2 and tax form generation
commonRisks: Payroll calculation errors affecting employee pay
Late or incorrect tax remittances causing penalties
Non-compliance with state wage and hour laws
Data security breach of payroll PII
FLSA misclassification of exempt vs. non-exempt employees
Garnishment processing errors creating legal liability
commonIntegrations: State and federal tax filing systems
complianceConsiderations: IRS payroll tax regulations (FICA, FUTA, federal withholding)
State income tax and unemployment insurance requirements
FLSA overtime and minimum wage requirements
ACA reporting for applicable large employers
Child support and garnishment laws
starterPromptContext: You are working on a payroll or HR operations platform where accuracy, compliance, and timeliness are non-negotiable. Errors directly impact employees and create regulatory exposure. Key stakeholders include payroll administrators, finance teams, HR business partners, and compliance officers.

name: Banking / Payments
description: Banking and payments platforms support deposit account management, money movement, card processing, and digital banking experiences for retail and commercial customers. This domain encompasses core banking modernization, real-time payments (RTP, FedNow), and embedded finance capabilities built on modern APIs. Products in this space must meet the highest standards of reliability, security, and regulatory compliance while delivering the seamless digital experiences customers now expect.
commonWorkflows: ACH, wire, and real-time payment initiation
Bank reconciliation and general ledger posting
commonRisks: Payment system outages affecting customer access to funds
Fraud and money laundering through payment channels
Regulatory enforcement actions from banking regulators
Core banking system modernization risk
API security vulnerabilities in open banking
Operational errors in high-value wire processing
commonIntegrations: Core banking systems (FIS, Fiserv, Jack Henry)
Payment networks (Visa, Mastercard, ACH, RTP, FedNow)
Open banking APIs and fintech partner integrations
complianceConsiderations: BSA/AML program requirements and FinCEN rules
Regulation E for consumer electronic fund transfers
OCC and FDIC safety and soundness examination standards
PCI DSS for card data
FFIEC cybersecurity assessment framework
starterPromptContext: You are working in banking and payments technology where zero-downtime reliability and regulatory compliance are the highest priorities. Customers depend on these systems for access to their money. Key stakeholders include retail banking product teams, fraud operations, compliance officers, and technology leadership.

name: Wealth Management
description: Wealth management technology platforms support investment advisory, portfolio management, financial planning, and client relationship management for registered investment advisors (RIAs), broker-dealers, and private banks. The sector is evolving through digital advisory (robo-advisor) models, tax-optimized direct indexing, and unified wealth platforms that consolidate portfolio, planning, and client communication tools. Products must meet strict SEC/FINRA fiduciary and suitability requirements while delivering personalized client experiences.
commonUsers: High Net Worth Clients and Investors
commonRisks: Fiduciary breaches and suitability violations
Cybersecurity breaches of high-value client data
Model portfolio errors affecting client accounts
Regulatory changes to fiduciary standards
Data feed failures causing stale portfolio data
Advisor attrition taking client relationships
commonIntegrations: Portfolio management systems (Orion, Tamarac, Black Diamond)
CRM platforms (Salesforce Financial Services Cloud, Redtail)
complianceConsiderations: SEC Investment Advisers Act fiduciary requirements
FINRA suitability rules for broker-dealers
SEC Regulation Best Interest (Reg BI)
Form ADV and Form CRS disclosure requirements
AML program requirements for investment accounts
starterPromptContext: You are working in wealth management technology supporting financial advisors, portfolio managers, and high-net-worth clients. The domain requires strict regulatory compliance with SEC and FINRA rules while delivering personalized portfolio and planning experiences. Key metrics include AUM growth, advisor productivity, and client retention.

name: Claims Processing
description: Claims processing technology automates and manages the intake, investigation, adjudication, and payment of insurance, warranty, or benefits claims across health, property, casualty, and life insurance lines. Modern claims platforms leverage AI for automated adjudication of low-complexity claims, fraud scoring, and document extraction to reduce cycle times and improve accuracy. The domain demands strict audit trails, regulatory compliance, and integration with policy and payment systems.
commonUsers: Medical Reviewers and Nurse Case Managers
commonRisks: Fraudulent claims inflating loss ratios
Regulatory non-compliance with state prompt payment laws
AI adjudication errors on complex claims
Data privacy breaches of sensitive medical or financial data
Litigation risk from improper claim denials
Integration failures between claims and policy systems
complianceConsiderations: State prompt payment laws and claim settlement timeframes
HIPAA for medical claims and PHI
State unfair claims settlement practices acts
NAIC claims handling guidelines
GDPR for European claimant data
starterPromptContext: You are working on claims processing technology where speed, accuracy, and fraud prevention are the core priorities. The business measures success by claim cycle time, straight-through processing rates, and LAE reduction. Key stakeholders include claims adjusters, fraud analysts, compliance teams, and claimants.

name: Field Service Management
description: Field service management platforms coordinate the scheduling, dispatch, and execution of on-site service work performed by technicians at customer locations across industries including HVAC, telecom, utilities, medical equipment, and IT services. Modern FSM systems use AI-powered scheduling optimization, mobile technician apps, and IoT-connected asset management to improve first-time fix rates and technician utilization. Products must serve dispatchers, field technicians, and customers with real-time work order management and customer communication.
commonWorkflows: Work order creation and job scheduling
Mobile work order execution and documentation
Asset and equipment service history management
commonRisks: Missed SLAs due to technician scheduling failures
Parts unavailability causing repeat visits
Technician safety incidents on customer sites
Poor mobile app reliability in low-connectivity areas
Customer data privacy in on-site access management
Inaccurate asset records leading to wrong parts dispatched
commonIntegrations: ERP and service management systems (SAP FSM, ServiceMax)
Mapping and route optimization (Google Maps, HERE)
complianceConsiderations: OSHA safety requirements for field work
Customer site access and security protocols
Industry-specific regulations (HIPAA for medical device service)
Vehicle fleet safety and DOT compliance
Data privacy for customer site access records
starterPromptContext: You are working in field service management technology where the goal is improving technician utilization, first-time fix rates, and customer satisfaction for on-site service operations. Key stakeholders include dispatchers, field technicians, service managers, and customers. Success is measured by SLA compliance, first-time fix rate, and CSAT.
